name: Philippines
tagline: From barangays and spice-trade sultanates to Asia's first republic and the home of People Power.
summary: The Philippines is an archipelago of more than seven thousand islands whose history runs from Austronesian seafarers and the autonomous barangays of the datus, through the Indianised polities and the Islamic sultanates of Sulu and Maguindanao, into three and a half centuries of Spanish rule begun by Legazpi in 1565 and bound to the world by the Manila-Acapulco galleon trade. A national awakening led by Jose Rizal and the Katipunan of Andres Bonifacio erupted in revolution in 1896, and on 12 June 1898 Emilio Aguinaldo proclaimed independence, only for Spain to cede the islands to the United States. After a brutal Philippine-American War, an American colonial period and the Japanese occupation, the Republic of the Philippines won full independence on 4 July 1946. The young democracy endured the Marcos dictatorship and martial law until the peaceful People Power Revolution of 1986 restored it, and the modern republic endures to 2026.

founding.label: Independence of the Republic of the Philippines
founding.detail: On 4 July 1946 the United States formally relinquished sovereignty over the Philippines and recognised the independence of the Republic of the Philippines under the Treaty of Manila, with Manuel Roxas as its first president. Filipinos count their independence from the 12 June 1898 declaration by Emilio Aguinaldo, and in 1962 moved the national Independence Day to that date; but full sovereignty came in 1946, after Spanish colonial rule, the Philippine-American War, the American period and the devastation of the Japanese occupation.

quickFacts.label: Declaration of independence
quickFacts.value: 12 June 1898, by Emilio Aguinaldo at Kawit, Cavite

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: Archipelago of over 7,000 islands; Luzon, Visayas, Mindanao

quickFacts.label: Predominant religion
quickFacts.value: Roman Catholicism, rooted in the Spanish period; Muslim south

eras.title: Barangays, Indianised Polities and the Islamic Sultanates
eras.standfirst: Before Spanish ships arrived, the archipelago was a mosaic of autonomous barangays led by datus, linked into a wider Southeast Asian maritime world, with powerful Islamic sultanates rising in the south.
body: Long settled by Austronesian peoples, the pre-colonial Philippines was organised into hundreds of small, largely autonomous communities called barangays, each headed by a chief or datu and bound by ties of kinship, trade and tribute. There was no single state; instead, coastal settlements such as Tondo, Maynila and the Visayan polities traded gold, forest products and slaves across a network reaching China, the Malay world and beyond. The Laguna Copperplate Inscription, dated to 900 CE and now held by the National Museum, is the earliest known Philippine document and shows a society of ranked nobles, freemen and dependents already connected to the wider region.

Indian religious and political ideas, transmitted through Malay and Javanese contacts, shaped the courts and vocabularies of these polities, even though no great temple-states arose as they did in Java. From the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries a new and transforming force arrived from the south: Islam, carried by traders and missionaries along the same sea lanes that had long bound the islands to maritime Southeast Asia. In the Sulu archipelago and Mindanao, conversion reshaped politics and gave rise to enduring sultanates.

The Sultanate of Sulu, traditionally founded in the mid-fifteenth century with its seat at Jolo, grew into a formidable maritime state controlling trade across the Sulu Sea and exerting influence as far as Borneo. On Mindanao, the Sultanate of Maguindanao consolidated under rulers descended from the missionary Sharif Muhammad Kabungsuwan and reached its height under Sultan Kudarat in the seventeenth century. These Muslim states, unlike the northern barangays, would resist Spanish conquest for centuries, leaving Mindanao and Sulu a distinct and contested frontier.

events.title: The Laguna Copperplate Inscription
events.summary: Dated to 900 CE, the earliest known Philippine document records a debt remission and reveals an organised society of datus and nobles already linked to the wider Malay and Javanese world.

events.title: Founding of the Sultanate of Sulu
events.summary: Islam takes political form as the Sultanate of Sulu rises around Jolo, becoming a powerful maritime state controlling trade across the Sulu Sea and toward Borneo.

events.title: Islam consolidates in Maguindanao
events.summary: The conversion of ruling families on Mindanao, associated with the missionary Sharif Muhammad Kabungsuwan, founds the Sultanate of Maguindanao, which expands through the 16th and 17th centuries.

figures.name: Sultan Kudarat
figures.blurb: The ruler under whom the Sultanate of Maguindanao reached the summit of its strength and influence on Mindanao, leading sustained resistance to Spanish encroachment in the south.

eras.title: Spanish Colonisation, Catholicism and the Galleon Trade
eras.standfirst: From Legazpi's landing in 1565 Spain bound the archipelago into its empire, ruling for three centuries through the Church and the Manila-Acapulco galleon, the lifeline that linked Asia to the Americas.
body: Sustained Spanish colonisation began in 1565, when Miguel Lopez de Legazpi established the first permanent settlement at Cebu. In 1571 the Spanish founded Manila on the site of an existing Muslim settlement, making it the capital of a colony governed in the name of the Spanish crown but in practice administered from Mexico. By the end of the sixteenth century most of the coastal and lowland regions from Luzon to the northern Visayas were under Spanish control, while the southern sultanates of Sulu and Maguindanao held out.

The defining instrument of colonial society was the Roman Catholic Church. Spanish friars of the Augustinian, Franciscan, Jesuit, Dominican and Recollect orders converted the lowland population, organising villages around the parish church and making Catholicism the deepest and most lasting Spanish legacy in the islands. The squat, earthquake-resistant baroque churches built by Filipino and Chinese craftsmen, four of which are inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage list, still mark the landscape of the lowlands.

The colony's economic lifeline was the Manila-Acapulco galleon trade, which ran from 1565 to 1815. Each year galleons crossed the Pacific carrying Chinese silks, porcelain and spices from Manila to Acapulco in New Spain, returning laden with Mexican silver. This commerce sustained the Spanish in Manila, drew a large Chinese merchant community to the city, and tied the Philippines into the first truly global system of exchange linking Asia, the Americas and Europe.

events.title: Legazpi founds the first Spanish settlement at Cebu
events.summary: Miguel Lopez de Legazpi establishes Spain's first permanent settlement in the islands at Cebu, beginning more than three centuries of Spanish colonial rule.

events.title: Founding of Manila
events.summary: Legazpi establishes Manila as the capital of the Spanish colony; by the century's end most lowland Luzon and the Visayas are under Spanish control.

events.title: The Manila-Acapulco galleon trade
events.summary: For 250 years Spanish galleons exchange Chinese silks for Mexican silver across the Pacific, making Manila a hub of global commerce and an economic lifeline for the colony.

figures.name: Miguel Lopez de Legazpi
figures.blurb: Led the expedition that founded the first permanent Spanish settlement at Cebu in 1565 and the capital of Manila in 1571, establishing Spanish rule over the archipelago.

pullquote.text: The galleons were the sole means of communication between Spain and its Philippine colony and served as an economic lifeline for the Spaniards in Manila.

eras.title: Rizal, the Katipunan and the Revolution of 1896-1898
eras.standfirst: A reform movement led by Jose Rizal gave way to the armed Katipunan of Andres Bonifacio; the 1896 revolution and Rizal's execution made Spanish rule untenable, and on 12 June 1898 Aguinaldo proclaimed independence.
body: By the late nineteenth century an educated Filipino class, the ilustrados, was pressing for reform within the Spanish system. Its leading voice was the physician and novelist Jose Rizal, whose novels Noli Me Tangere (1886) and El Filibusterismo (1891) exposed colonial and clerical abuses and stirred national consciousness. Rizal founded the reformist La Liga Filipina in 1892, seeking peaceful change rather than revolt, and was promptly arrested and exiled by the Spanish authorities.

When reform stalled, Andres Bonifacio founded the Katipunan in 1892, a secret revolutionary society pledged to win independence by force. In August 1896 the discovery of the conspiracy triggered open revolt, dramatised by the 'Cry' in which Katipuneros tore up their residence certificates in defiance of Spain. On 30 December 1896 the Spanish executed Rizal by firing squad on charges of fomenting rebellion; his martyrdom enraged and united Filipinos, making continued Spanish rule impossible.

Leadership of the revolution passed to Emilio Aguinaldo, who eclipsed and, after a bitter internal split, ordered the execution of Bonifacio in 1897. When the Spanish-American War broke out in 1898 and an American squadron destroyed the Spanish fleet in Manila Bay, Aguinaldo returned from exile, rallied his forces, and on 12 June 1898 proclaimed the independence of the Philippines from the window of his house at Kawit, Cavite. A revolutionary government and, in 1899, the Malolos Republic followed, with Aguinaldo as president of Asia's first constitutional republic.

events.title: Founding of the Katipunan
events.summary: Andres Bonifacio establishes the secret revolutionary society the Katipunan, dedicated to winning Philippine independence from Spain by armed force.

events.title: Outbreak of the Philippine Revolution
events.summary: The discovery of the Katipunan sparks open revolt; Katipuneros proclaim the 'Cry' of defiance, beginning the armed revolution against Spanish rule.

events.title: Execution of Jose Rizal
events.summary: Spain executes the reformist writer Jose Rizal by firing squad in Manila; his martyrdom galvanises Filipinos and makes Spanish retention of the colony untenable.

events.title: Declaration of Philippine independence
events.summary: Emilio Aguinaldo proclaims independence from Spain at Kawit, Cavite, founding a provisional republic; this date later becomes the national Independence Day.

figures.name: Jose Rizal
figures.role: National hero, reformist writer and physician
figures.blurb: The ilustrado whose novels Noli Me Tangere and El Filibusterismo exposed colonial abuses and awakened Filipino nationalism. Executed by Spain in 1896, he became the country's foremost national hero.

figures.name: Andres Bonifacio
figures.role: Founder and supremo of the Katipunan
figures.blurb: Founder of the Katipunan and 'Father of the Philippine Revolution', who launched the 1896 revolt. He was executed in 1897 amid a power struggle with Aguinaldo's faction.

pullquote.text: Rizal's public execution on 30 December 1896 so enraged and united Filipinos as to make permanent retention of power by Spain clearly impossible.
pullquote.attribution: On the impact of Rizal's execution

eras.title: Cession to America, the Philippine-American War and the Commonwealth
eras.standfirst: Spain ceded the islands to the United States, and the betrayed Filipino republic fought a bloody war for independence; defeat ushered in an American colonial period that culminated in the self-governing Commonwealth.
body: Filipinos were not consulted at the Treaty of Paris of December 1898, by which Spain ceded the Philippines, Puerto Rico and Guam to the United States. Having fought for independence, Aguinaldo's republic now faced a new colonial power. On 4 February 1899, two days before the U.S. Senate ratified the treaty, fighting broke out between American forces and Filipino nationalists, opening the Philippine-American War.

The war was long and devastating. After conventional defeats, Aguinaldo's forces turned to guerrilla warfare; he was captured in 1901, and President Theodore Roosevelt declared the conflict over on 4 July 1902, though resistance continued in places. Over four thousand Americans and some twenty thousand Filipino combatants died, while as many as two hundred thousand Filipino civilians perished from violence, famine and disease, one of the great catastrophes of the era.

American rule then settled into a colonial period that combined exploitation with promises of eventual self-government. A Filipino elected assembly met from 1907, and the Jones Act of 1916 pledged future independence. In 1935 the archipelago became the Commonwealth of the Philippines, an autonomous, self-governing transitional state under American sovereignty, with Manuel L. Quezon as its first president and a ten-year path to full independence set out before the Second World War intervened.

events.title: Treaty of Paris: Spain cedes the Philippines to the U.S.
events.summary: By the Treaty of Paris ending the Spanish-American War, Spain cedes the Philippines to the United States without consulting Filipinos, who had already declared independence.

events.title: The Philippine-American War
events.summary: Filipino nationalists under Aguinaldo fight the United States for independence; the war kills thousands of combatants and as many as 200,000 civilians before ending in 1902.

events.title: Establishment of the Commonwealth
events.summary: The Philippines becomes a self-governing Commonwealth under U.S. sovereignty, with Manuel L. Quezon as first president and a ten-year transition toward full independence.

figures.name: Emilio Aguinaldo
figures.role: Revolutionary leader and first president of the Philippine Republic
figures.blurb: Led the revolution after Bonifacio's death, declared independence in 1898, and headed the Malolos Republic. He fought the United States in the Philippine-American War until his capture in 1901.

pullquote.text: As many as 200,000 Filipino civilians died from violence, famine, and disease.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Office of the Historian, on the Philippine-American War

eras.title: Japanese Occupation, Independence and the Marcos Dictatorship
eras.standfirst: The Japanese occupation laid the country in ruins; full independence came in 1946, but after a generation the republic was seized by Ferdinand Marcos, whose martial law became a fourteen-year dictatorship.
body: Japan invaded in December 1941, soon after Pearl Harbor, and occupied the Philippines from 1942 to 1945. The occupation was brutal: Filipinos waged a determined guerrilla resistance, but the war and the battle to retake the islands left over a million Filipinos dead out of a population of eighteen million, with Manila and most major cities reduced to ruins. It remains the most destructive episode in the country's modern history.

On 4 July 1946 the United States relinquished sovereignty and recognised the independence of the Republic of the Philippines under the Treaty of Manila, with Manuel Roxas as first president of the new republic. The young state faced enormous reconstruction, an early communist Huk rebellion, and deep social divisions, yet it built a functioning electoral democracy in the decades that followed independence.

That democracy was overturned by Ferdinand Marcos. Elected president in 1965 and re-elected in 1969, Marcos declared martial law under Proclamation No. 1081 on 21 September 1972, citing communist and Muslim insurgencies. He arrested opponents, muzzled the press, rewrote the constitution and ruled by decree, beginning a fourteen-year dictatorship marked by the arbitrary arrest of thousands, widespread human-rights abuses and vast corruption that enriched his family and cronies while the economy faltered.

events.title: Japanese occupation
events.summary: Japan occupies the Philippines during the Second World War; guerrilla resistance and the campaign to retake the islands leave over a million Filipinos dead and Manila in ruins.

events.title: Independence of the Republic of the Philippines
events.summary: The United States relinquishes sovereignty and recognises Philippine independence under the Treaty of Manila; Manuel Roxas becomes first president of the republic.

events.title: Marcos declares martial law
events.summary: Ferdinand Marcos imposes martial law under Proclamation No. 1081, arresting opponents and seizing one-man rule in a dictatorship that lasts until 1986.

figures.name: Ferdinand Marcos
figures.blurb: Elected president in 1965, he declared martial law in 1972 and ruled as a dictator for fourteen years, presiding over repression and large-scale corruption before being ousted in 1986.

pullquote.text: Over a million Filipinos had died, out of a population of 18 million, and Manila and most of the major cities were in ruins.

eras.title: People Power and the Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: In 1986 a peaceful uprising on EDSA toppled Marcos and restored democracy under Corazon Aquino; since then the republic has weathered coups, charismatic and dynastic leaders, and the return of the Marcos name to power.
body: When Marcos claimed victory in a fraudulent snap election in February 1986 against Corazon Aquino, widow of the assassinated opposition leader Benigno Aquino, the country erupted. From 22 to 25 February millions gathered on Epifanio de los Santos Avenue (EDSA) in Manila in a non-violent protest; defecting soldiers, the Catholic Church under Cardinal Jaime Sin, and ordinary citizens converged to protect the rebels. Faced with collapse, Marcos and his family fled into exile, and Corazon Aquino was sworn in as president, restoring democracy in a movement that became known worldwide as People Power.

Aquino's government promulgated a new constitution in 1987 and survived repeated coup attempts. The decades that followed brought a vigorous if turbulent democracy: presidents from Fidel Ramos to Joseph Estrada, who was driven from office in 2001, and Gloria Macapagal Arroyo. A persistent challenge has been the long Muslim separatist conflict in Mindanao, addressed through peace agreements that created the autonomous Bangsamoro region, alongside enduring problems of poverty, inequality and political dynasties.

In the twenty-first century the Philippines has been shaped by strong personalities and dynastic politics. Rodrigo Duterte, elected in 2016, waged a deadly anti-drug campaign that drew international condemnation. In 2022 Ferdinand 'Bongbong' Marcos Jr., son of the late dictator, won the presidency, returning his family to power thirty-six years after People Power. As ASEAN chair in 2026, the modern republic remains one of Asia's notable democracies, navigating economic growth, corruption scandals and contested maritime claims in the South China Sea.

events.title: People Power Revolution
events.summary: A peaceful uprising on EDSA, backed by defecting troops and the Catholic Church, drives Ferdinand Marcos into exile and installs Corazon Aquino as president, restoring democracy.

events.title: New democratic constitution
events.summary: Under President Corazon Aquino a new constitution is ratified, re-establishing democratic institutions, presidential term limits and a bill of rights after the Marcos dictatorship.

events.title: Return of the Marcos name to power
events.summary: Ferdinand 'Bongbong' Marcos Jr., son of the late dictator, wins the presidency, returning his family to the highest office 36 years after People Power.

figures.name: Corazon Aquino
figures.blurb: Widow of slain opposition leader Benigno Aquino, she became the figurehead of the 1986 People Power Revolution and the first female president of the Philippines, restoring democratic rule.

pullquote.text: Pressure from the international community, coupled with growing support for the opposition, forced Marcos and his wife Imelda to flee the country on 25 February 1986.

figures.name: Jose Rizal
figures.blurb: Author of Noli Me Tangere and El Filibusterismo whose writings awakened Filipino nationalism; his 1896 execution by Spain made him the country's foremost national hero.

figures.name: Emilio Aguinaldo
figures.role: First President of the Philippine Republic (1899-1901)
figures.blurb: Revolutionary leader who declared independence on 12 June 1898 and headed the Malolos Republic before fighting and losing the Philippine-American War to the United States.

figures.name: Corazon Aquino
figures.blurb: Leader of the 1986 People Power Revolution and the nation's first woman president, who restored democracy after the fall of the Marcos dictatorship.
